commentary: Section 301 exclusions are back in the news. If you import from China, pull your HTS list now and check which of your products are even eligible before the comment window closes. Most importers wait too long.
USMCA rule-of-origin is not a checkbox. The regional value content test trips up more auto-parts importers than any other thing I see. Document your BOM and supplier origins before you certify.
Year-end reminder for Ohio importers: reconcile your entries and confirm your classifications match what you actually shipped. CBP is paying attention to post-summary corrections.
Duty drawback is the refund most small importers never claim. If you export or destroy imported goods, you may be leaving real money on the table. Worth a 30-minute look.
New year, new tariff landscape. Build classification review into your Q1 plan, not your year-end scramble. A clean HTS database saves you penalties later.
ISF penalties are avoidable. File complete, file on time, and never file incomplete and amend to beat a deadline. That is how you draw a fine. Hold the entry and get the data right.
Talked tariffs at the Columbus Trade Association board this month. Section 301 and USMCA keep dominating the conversation for Central Ohio manufacturers. Plan for volatility.
Closing the year: importers, check that your customs broker actually reviewed your classifications and did not just roll last year's forward. Same product, new ruling, different duty.
Q1 trade outlook: expect continued movement on Section 301 and more USMCA origin scrutiny on automotive parts. If you source from Mexico, get your origin documentation tight now.
USMCA recordkeeping is not optional. Keep your origin support for five years. I see importers certify origin and then cannot produce the BOM when CBP asks. Fix that before it costs you.
Heading into Q4: Ohio importers, lock your year-end classification review and confirm your USMCA certifications are current. The 2027 tariff picture is going to demand planning, not reacting.
2027 tariff outlook for Ohio manufacturers: plan for continued Section 301 uncertainty and tighter USMCA enforcement. Build duty exposure into your sourcing decisions, not after them.

If you import auto parts, your HTS classification drives your duty rate and your USMCA eligibility. Get it wrong on paper and you carry the exposure on every entry until CBP notices. Review before you certify.
Tariff engineering is legal. Misdescribing your goods to dodge duty is not. The line is whether the product actually changed. Know the difference before someone sells you a shortcut.
First sale valuation can cut your duty base on multi-tier import transactions. Most small importers never look at it. If you buy through a middleman, it is worth an afternoon with your broker.
Holiday import season is when classification mistakes compound. High volume, tight deadlines, and brokers rolling last year's codes forward. Spot-check your entries now, not in January.
USMCA certifications are not set-and-forget. A new supplier, a new BOM, a new production source can break your origin claim. Re-verify every year or be ready to prove it when asked.
ISF is ten data elements and a deadline. Importers treat it like paperwork until a $5,000 penalty shows up. File it complete, file it early, and stop amending to beat the clock.
Drawback takes patience and clean records, but the refund is real money on exported or destroyed goods. If you move volume, the math usually works. Run it once and see.
End of year: confirm your customs broker actually reviewed your classifications this cycle. Same product, new ruling, different duty. Do not assume last year's codes still hold.
Q1 is the time to fix your HTS database, not your year-end scramble. Clean classifications now save you penalties and post-summary corrections later. Build the review into your plan.
Spring reminder for Ohio importers: keep your USMCA origin support for five years. I see firms certify and then come up empty when CBP asks for the BOM. Fix the recordkeeping before it costs you.
Heading into year-end: lock your classification review and confirm your origin documentation is current. The 2027 tariff picture rewards planning and punishes reacting.

Closing the books for the year. Reconcile your entries, confirm your classifications match what you shipped, and start 2027 with a clean HTS list. The work you do now is the audit defense you want later.

USMCA rules of origin update: the automotive core parts threshold shifts we've been tracking are now guidance-final. If your BOM sources across the border, revisit your certification stack before Q1 filings.
Ohio importers: CBP just published new HTS classification guidance on sensor assemblies. Ohio Sensor Systems and Meridian Auto Parts clients — we already flagged three SKUs that shift subheadings.

Section 301 exclusion renewal window is 45 days out. If you have covered goods still exposed, get your renewal package moving now. We're helping Riverside Steel Trading and Toledo Brake Systems through theirs.
18 years in and the customs bar keeps evolving. Grateful for the Columbus trade community and the mentors who taught me to read a tariff schedule at 3 AM.
Announcing the fall cohort of the Columbus Trade Mentorship Program with Ryan Montoya at Montoya Logistics. Six new mentees this cycle — Jordan, Carla, Devin, Sofia, Natalie, and Andre. Welcome.
Congrats to Brandon Kim on passing his first solo ISF filing this month at Kim Trade Services. Second-generation customs work runs deep in our family.
Great Lakes Shipping Co filed their annual 232 renewal today with zero exceptions. Twelfth year running for this client — that's what compliance discipline looks like.
Speaking at the Columbus Trade Association board meeting Oct 15 on FDA prior notice remediation. If you're an importer of consumer packaged goods, this session is for you.
Cardinal Grain Cooperative closed their USMCA re-certification cycle two weeks early. Iris Whitaker and her team run one of the tightest ag export ops in central Ohio.
Reminder: EEI/AES post-departure filings are due 5 business days after export. We keep seeing importers miss this. Set up the automation, don't rely on memory.
Buckeye Textile Group's ISF 10+2 exception rate dropped to zero this quarter. Diane Ramos and the compliance team put in the work. Proud to be their broker.
The bonded warehouse strategy we built for Ohio Sensor Systems saved them $84K on Q3 landed cost. Sometimes the answer isn't a different HTS code — it's a different customs vehicle.
Heartland Ag Exports closed a drawback filing this week that recovered $127K in duties paid over 36 months. Wesley Palmer, patience pays.
Columbus Trade Mentorship Program applications for the winter cohort open Dec 1. If you're 0-3 years into customs work, apply. If you have 15+ years, mentor.
C-TPAT annual review season is here. If you're a Tier 2 or Tier 3 partner, your security profile updates are due by year end. Don't let this slip.
Congrats to Priya Anand on completing her Certified Customs Specialist recert. Marcus Webb, you're next this spring.
Meridian Auto Parts closed a Section 301 exclusion filing that clears the way for 2027 sourcing decisions. Raymond Foster — this one was a marathon.
Toledo Brake Systems' 232 exclusion renewal is in the queue for December. Marissa Coyle, thank you for the clean documentation.
The FDA prior notice remediation package we built for Buckeye Fresh Produce dropped their hold rate from 12% to under 1%. Rachel Nunez, that's a real number.
Twenty-plus years watching Great Lakes shipping evolve. The Seaway manifest coordination work we do for Great Lakes Shipping Co never gets old.
Ohio Bearings LLC's CBP 5106 update is complete. Anthony Rivera, your compliance file is now the cleanest in the industry.
Cleveland Ag Direct's phyto certificate coordination is running smoothly. Tomas Molina, congrats on the Q3 export volume increase.
Central Ohio Grain's USMCA cert cycle is on track. Vera Sullivan, thanks for the tight paperwork.
Columbus Precision Machining's HTS binding ruling came back favorable. Alan Whitfield, that's a $200K annual savings unlocked.
Riverside Steel Trading's Section 232 exclusion package is filed. Gregory Mahoney, we appreciate the collaboration.
If you're managing a customs program in central Ohio and want to talk process, DM me. Always happy to trade notes with fellow trade professionals.
Second-generation customs family. Brandon just filed his 50th entry solo. Not bragging, just proud.
Duty drawback: still the most under-utilized customs tool in the U.S. book. If you export what you import, you're leaving money on the table.
Free trade zone advisory work is picking up. If you're evaluating an FTZ for your Ohio operation, we can walk you through the math.
USMCA is not going anywhere in 2026. Build your compliance program around it, not around headlines.
Antidumping duty deposit reviews: if you have goods from covered sources, budget for the AR administrative review cycle. It always takes longer than you think.
Bonded warehouse strategy is having a moment. Every client asking about landed cost optimization ends up in the same conversation.
The Columbus Trade Association board meeting on Oct 15 will cover the 2027 outlook. If you're an Ohio importer or exporter, be there.
Origin determination binding rulings: worth the investment for high-volume SKUs. Don't guess your HTS.
Congrats to the Columbus Trade Mentorship Program winter cohort — six new mentees join us in January.
18 years at the customs bar. Still the best job I've ever had.
Grateful to be part of the Columbus trade community. Onward.
Section 321 de minimis strategy is shifting. If you're an e-commerce importer, get advice.
Customs bond renewal advisory: we walk clients through the math every year. It matters.
FDA prior notice remediation is a specialty. If your hold rate is high, call us.
Ohio Bearings, Cleveland Ag Direct, Central Ohio Grain — the mid-market clients that make this work rewarding.
Watching a Brandon Kim entry filing get released with zero exceptions is the parent moment I didn't know I needed.
USMCA re-certification cycles are the yearly rhythm of this practice.
CBP CROSS memos: bookmark the search page. It's the fastest way to check a binding ruling.

Reading The Wreck of the Edmund Fitzgerald again. Great Lakes shipping history never gets old.

Family dinner at 6:30 sharp. That routine has held for 25 years and I'm not changing it.
